subcategory_name_en: Nuclear Restarts, Life Extension & Nuclear Supply Chain
subcategory_name_ja: 原発再稼働・運転延長・原子力サプライチェーン
money_flow: There is no single cheque for this. The money shows up as utility spending: restarting idled reactors, paying for safety upgrades the regulator now requires, long-term maintenance, and early research into next-generation reactors. The companies that receive it are the regional power utilities that own reactors and the heavy-equipment and engineering firms that service them.
stock_logic: The plan sets a goal, not a guarantee. Nuclear supplied about 8.5% of Japan's electricity in 2023; the target is roughly 20% by 2040. Each restart still needs the Nuclear Regulation Authority to clear it, so the real signal is approvals at specific plants. The clearest near-term watch item is whether Tokyo Electric's Kashiwazaki-Kariwa units are allowed to restart.

subcategory_name_en: Grid Expansion, Interconnection & Power System Reform
subcategory_name_ja: 送電網増強・地域間連系・電力システム改革
money_flow: The grid operators' coordinating body, OCCTO, has costed the build at roughly ¥6 trillion to ¥7.9 trillion by 2050. The single largest piece is an undersea high-voltage cable from Hokkaido to Honshu, estimated at ¥1.5–1.8 trillion. Utilities recover this spending through regulated charges on electricity use, so the cash reaches the makers of cables, transformers, and substations over many years.
stock_logic: Because the cost is recovered through regulated charges rather than a one-off subsidy, the order flow is steadier but slower than a stimulus package. The signal to watch is OCCTO's master-plan updates and the tenders for specific lines, especially the Hokkaido–Honshu link. Whether construction holds to schedule, given the same labor shortage hitting all construction, is the open question.

subcategory_name_en: Renewables: Offshore Wind, Solar, Geothermal & Biomass
subcategory_name_ja: 再生可能エネルギー（洋上風力・太陽光・地熱・バイオマス）
money_flow: Two mechanisms carry the money. First, a surcharge on everyone's electricity bill funds above-market prices for renewable power, which pays developers and their equipment suppliers. Second, the government runs auctions for offshore-wind zones; winning a zone gives a developer the right to build, which then flows orders to turbine, foundation, and marine-construction firms.
stock_logic: The plan targets renewables at 40–50% of the power mix by 2040, with solar at 23–29% of that. For offshore wind specifically, the signal is each auction round and the designation of new promotion zones. A separate watch item is whether next-generation thin-film (perovskite) solar, which Japan is backing, reaches commercial scale.

subcategory_name_en: Hydrogen, Ammonia & Low-Carbon Fuels
subcategory_name_ja: 水素・アンモニア・低炭素燃料
money_flow: The Hydrogen Society Promotion Act, passed in May 2024, closes that price gap directly. The government, through the agency JOGMEC, pays approved suppliers the difference between what clean hydrogen costs to make and what buyers would pay for the dirty fuel it replaces. The commitment is about ¥3 trillion over fifteen years. The money reaches trading houses, plant engineers, gas and power utilities, and shipping firms.
stock_logic: This is a long, contracted subsidy, not a one-year grant. The government has committed the ¥3 trillion and opened the first round of applications, and has already certified ammonia projects led by JERA and Mitsui. The open question is whether enough clean hydrogen is actually produced and bought at scale. Watch the certified-project list and the first delivered volumes.

subcategory_name_en: CCS, CCUS & Carbon Management Infrastructure
subcategory_name_ja: CCS・CCUS・炭素管理インフラ
money_flow: Japan passed the CCS Business Act in 2024, which created the licences and rules for storing carbon underground. The government, again through JOGMEC, selected a set of advanced projects and supports the capture, transport, and storage steps. The money reaches engineering firms, pipeline and shipping operators, and the heavy industries that emit the carbon.
stock_logic: The policy has moved from idea to law, with a stated aim of starting commercial CCS by 2030. Because no single headline budget has been set, the signal is project-level: which advanced projects reach a final investment decision, and whether storage sites are licensed on time. Treat the 2030 start date as an ambition to test, not a delivered fact.

subcategory_name_en: Carbon Pricing, GX-ETS & Transition Finance
subcategory_name_ja: カーボンプライシング・GX-ETS・トランジションファイナンス
money_flow: The government issued about ¥20 trillion of 'GX transition bonds' — borrowing now to fund decarbonization investment — with the aim of pulling in ¥150 trillion of public and private investment over ten years from 2023. From FY2026 a full emissions-trading scheme (GX-ETS) begins, and fossil-fuel charges follow later. So banks, exchanges, and clean-tech suppliers benefit, while heavy emitters face a rising cost.
stock_logic: This splits the market into winners and cost-bearers, which is the point. The government has committed the ¥20 trillion and set FY2026 for the trading scheme to start in earnest. The open questions are how high the carbon price actually rises and how strictly the scheme is enforced — a weak price would blunt the whole mechanism. Watch the GX-ETS rulebook and the first compliance cycle.

subcategory_name_en: Stand-Off Missiles & Counterstrike Capability
subcategory_name_ja: スタンド・オフ・ミサイル／反撃能力
money_flow: This is one of the clearest spending buckets in Japan's five-year, ¥43 trillion Defense Buildup Program (fiscal 2023–2027). About ¥5 trillion of that program is set aside for counterstrike and stand-off weapons. The money is committed through multi-year procurement contracts, so it flows steadily to missile makers, propulsion and guidance suppliers, and electronics firms.
stock_logic: Unlike most policy themes, this one is already contracted, so the question is delivery rather than approval. The government is buying an upgraded, longer-range version of the domestic Type-12 missile and begins mass-producing home-made hypersonic missiles from FY2026. Watch the annual defense budget (requested each August, approved each December) and production milestones for those two programs.

subcategory_name_en: Integrated Air & Missile Defense
subcategory_name_ja: 統合防空ミサイル防衛
money_flow: It is a named priority inside the same ¥43 trillion Defense Buildup Program. The biggest single project is two new Aegis System Equipped Vessels — large warships built around a missile-defense radar and interceptor system — plus a glide-phase interceptor being co-developed with the United States. The money reaches shipbuilders, radar and electronics makers, and interceptor suppliers.
stock_logic: The spending is committed, so the watch items are build milestones: the construction schedule of the two Aegis vessels and procurement decisions on interceptors. Because these are long warship programs, progress is measured in years, and delays show up as deferred revenue for the contractors rather than cancellations.

subcategory_name_en: Unmanned Systems, Drones & Counter-UAS
subcategory_name_ja: 無人機・ドローン・対UAS
money_flow: Funding sits inside the ¥43 trillion program, and the FY2026 budget expanded it. The plan aims for practical deployment of drones by FY2027, and the military is buying and leasing systems early rather than waiting for full domestic development. The money reaches drone makers, sensor and communications suppliers, and the firms building counter-drone defenses.
stock_logic: The government has set FY2027 as the target for practical use and increased the budget line, so the near-term signals are acquisition rounds and counter-drone deployments. A reasonable check is whether Japan buys mostly foreign systems first or succeeds in building a domestic supply base — the investable outcome depends on which.

subcategory_name_en: Defense Production Base, Munitions & Sustainment
subcategory_name_ja: 防衛生産基盤・弾薬・継戦能力
money_flow: The Defense Production Base Strengthening Act, in force from 2023, created support to reinforce production lines, fund the transfer of equipment businesses between firms, and expand munitions capacity. Combined with the larger ¥43 trillion program, this steadies orders for prime contractors, munitions and explosives makers, shipyards, and the smaller specialist suppliers beneath them.
stock_logic: The shift is from one-off purchases to sustained capacity, so the signal is whether suppliers actually invest in new lines — for example, expanded munitions plants — rather than just bidding on single contracts. Watch production-base reinforcement grants and announced capacity expansions.

subcategory_name_en: Economic Security Supply Chains & Specified Critical Products
subcategory_name_ja: 経済安全保障サプライチェーン・特定重要物資
money_flow: The government subsidizes companies that build or secure domestic supply of these named products, and asks them to submit supply-security plans it can approve and back. The money reaches the makers of strategic components and materials, the trading houses that source minerals, and battery and machine-tool suppliers.
stock_logic: This is a subsidy-driven theme, so the signal is which products get added to the 'specified critical' list and which company supply-security plans are approved. Because the same act also funds chips and cloud (see those sub-themes), it is best read alongside the semiconductor and AI-compute pages rather than on its own.

subcategory_name_en: Sovereign Cloud, AI Compute & Secure Digital Infrastructure
subcategory_name_ja: ソブリンクラウド・AI計算基盤・セキュアデジタル基盤
money_flow: Using the Economic Security Promotion Act, the government approved up to ¥72.5 billion in an early round of subsidies for domestic cloud and AI-computing capacity, and has signalled more. The money reaches domestic cloud providers, data-center builders, telecom carriers, and the power and cooling suppliers that data centers depend on.
stock_logic: This overlaps heavily with the AI-compute and data-center sub-themes; here the lens is security and self-sufficiency. The government has committed the first ¥72.5 billion and indicated further rounds. The check is whether Japanese providers win the work, or whether foreign hyperscalers end up supplying most of the capacity even when it sits on Japanese soil.

subcategory_name_en: Advanced Logic Semiconductors & Foundry Revival
subcategory_name_ja: 先端ロジック半導体・ファウンドリ復活
money_flow: The state is funding this directly and heavily. Cumulative government support for Rapidus is heading toward roughly ¥2.9 trillion, part of a broader ¥10 trillion national pledge for semiconductors and AI by 2030 (with about ¥1.23 trillion in the FY2026 budget alone). The money reaches Rapidus and, more investably for listed names, the equipment and materials suppliers that sell into its lines.
stock_logic: This is a bet that may or may not pay off, so separate the commitment from the result. The government has committed the money; what is not yet proven is whether Rapidus can reach high-volume 2nm production with good yields and win paying customers. Watch the funding tranches, pilot-line yield news, and any customer or partner announcements. The company targets profitability around FY2030 and a stock-market listing around FY2031.

subcategory_name_en: Legacy, Power, Analog & MCU Semiconductors
subcategory_name_ja: レガシー・パワー・アナログ・MCU半導体
money_flow: Under the economic-security framework, the government subsidizes companies that expand domestic capacity for power, analog, and microcontroller chips. The money reaches power-semiconductor and silicon-carbide makers and the automotive-chip suppliers building new lines.
stock_logic: This is a more grounded bet than leading-edge logic because Japan already has the customers and the know-how. The signal is which capacity-expansion subsidies are awarded and whether the new silicon-carbide lines ramp on schedule as electric-vehicle demand grows. A useful check is order trends from auto and industrial customers, which show whether the new capacity is actually being used.

subcategory_name_en: Semiconductor Materials, Chemicals & Advanced Components
subcategory_name_ja: 半導体材料・化学品・先端部品
money_flow: Policy here reinforces an existing strength rather than building a new one. Under economic-security rules, the government has approved supply-security plans for semiconductor materials, helping makers expand capacity. Demand also comes directly from the new fabs being built in Japan, such as TSMC's Kumamoto plant and Rapidus. The money and orders reach wafer, photoresist, substrate, and specialty-chemical makers.
stock_logic: Because Japan already leads here, the question is capacity and share rather than catch-up. Watch which materials get supply-security designations and whether the new domestic fabs lift local materials demand. A clean check is the capacity-expansion announcements from the leading wafer and resist suppliers.

subcategory_name_en: Semiconductor Manufacturing Equipment & Precision Tools
subcategory_name_ja: 半導体製造装置・精密機械
money_flow: Policy works two ways here. Domestic support strengthens local equipment capacity, while US-led export controls restrict what Japanese makers can sell to China. So the same companies enjoy strong demand from new fabs worldwide and the AI-chip boom, but carry the risk that a large export market is fenced off. The money reaches etch, deposition, cleaning, inspection, and test-equipment makers.
stock_logic: Demand and policy push in opposite directions, so track both. The upside signal is the global wave of fab spending and rising demand to test AI chips; the downside signal is each tightening of China export controls. A practical check is the China revenue share these makers disclose, which shows how exposed each is.

subcategory_name_en: Advanced Packaging, Chiplets & Heterogeneous Integration
subcategory_name_ja: 先端パッケージング・チップレット・ヘテロジニアス集積
money_flow: Japan's Post-5G support program funds advanced-packaging and chiplet technology, and demand comes directly from the AI hardware boom. The money and orders reach package-substrate makers, bonding and inspection-equipment suppliers, and outsourced assembly-and-test providers.
stock_logic: This is one of the faster-growing parts of the chip supply chain, so the question is capacity rather than demand. Watch advanced-packaging research grants and the substrate makers' capacity announcements, since AI demand is currently outrunning supply. A check is whether substrate suppliers raise capex guidance.

subcategory_name_en: AI Compute, Data Centers & Semiconductor Demand Pull
subcategory_name_ja: AI計算・データセンター・半導体需要創出
money_flow: Part of the ¥10 trillion chips-and-AI pledge funds domestic AI computing, and a separate round approved up to ¥72.5 billion for cloud and AI-compute capacity. Beyond the chipmakers, the spending reaches data-center builders, power and cooling suppliers, telecom carriers, and the firms that connect it all with optical cable.
stock_logic: The clearest signal is the wave of data-center investment landing in Japan and the demand to test AI chips. Watch the cloud and AI-compute subsidy rounds and capital-spending plans from global cloud operators in Japan. A useful check is power-grid connection approvals, since electricity — not money — is the binding constraint.

subcategory_name_en: Factory Automation, Robotics & Labor-Saving Manufacturing
subcategory_name_ja: ファクトリーオートメーション・ロボット・省人化製造
money_flow: The government funds labor-saving investment directly. The 2025 New Capitalism plan includes a labor-saving investment promotion plan with subsidies that help smaller firms buy automation. Demand also comes from reshoring (see domestic investment) and the new fabs. The money and orders reach industrial-robot, machine-tool, sensor, and factory-software makers.
stock_logic: This is both a labor policy and an industrial one, so it has a steady, structural pull rather than a one-off budget. Watch take-up of the labor-saving subsidies and capital-spending orders from manufacturers. A check is the order books of the leading robot and sensor makers, which reveal whether the automation push is translating into actual purchases.

subcategory_name_en: Strategic Domestic Investment, Reshoring & Plant Subsidies
subcategory_name_ja: 戦略的国内投資・国内回帰・工場補助
money_flow: The revised Industrial Competitiveness Enhancement Act supports domestic production in strategic areas through grants, production-linked tax credits, and loans. The clearest example is the public money pulling chip fabs to Japan. The spending reaches construction and engineering firms, factory-equipment makers, and the regional manufacturers and utilities around new plants.
stock_logic: Reshoring is real where the subsidy is large (chips) and slower elsewhere. The signal is which strategic business plans get approved and where new plants are sited. A grounded check is construction orders at the firms that build fabs and plants — the early, visible beneficiaries before the factories themselves produce anything.

subcategory_name_en: AI Compute, GPU Cloud & Sovereign AI Infrastructure
subcategory_name_ja: AI計算・GPUクラウド・ソブリンAI基盤
money_flow: The government is funding it directly. It approved up to ¥72.5 billion for domestic cloud and AI computing, and budgeted around ¥387.3 billion to develop home-grown foundation models and data infrastructure, all within the wider ¥10 trillion chips-and-AI pledge. The money reaches cloud providers, systems integrators, telecom carriers, and supercomputing operators.
stock_logic: The commitment is set; the open question is whether domestic players build a real position or simply resell foreign technology. Watch the AI-compute subsidy rounds and the GENIAC program for domestic foundation models. A useful check is whether Japanese-built models and clouds win meaningful enterprise and government work.

subcategory_name_en: Data Centers, Green Data Centers & Watt-Bit Infrastructure
subcategory_name_ja: データセンター・グリーンDC・ワットビット連携
money_flow: The pull here is mostly private investment, supported by policy that eases siting and links data centers to power supply. The spending reaches data-center operators and real-estate developers, construction firms, power and cooling-equipment makers, and fiber and telecom suppliers.
stock_logic: This is largely a demand story, with policy smoothing the path. Watch regional siting incentives and the capital-spending plans of global cloud operators in Japan. The binding check is power: grid-connection approvals and local electricity availability decide how many of the announced data centers actually get built.

subcategory_name_en: Government Cloud, Public-Sector IT Modernization & GovTech
subcategory_name_ja: ガバメントクラウド・行政IT近代化・GovTech
money_flow: This is a direct public-procurement pipeline: government pays vendors to migrate systems and run them in the cloud. The money reaches IT-services firms, cloud-migration specialists, certified software vendors, and cybersecurity providers. Local governments face deadlines to move their core systems, which creates a multi-year stream of contracts.
stock_logic: Because it runs on procurement and legal deadlines, this is steadier and more visible than most digital themes. Watch the local-government migration deadlines and which vendors get onto the Government Cloud and the approved-software lists. A clean check is the public-sector order backlog the leading systems integrators report.

subcategory_name_en: Digital Payments, Fintech Infrastructure & Cashless Economy
subcategory_name_ja: デジタル決済・フィンテック基盤・キャッシュレス
money_flow: This is a target-and-standards policy, not a spending program. The government sets goals and nudges merchants to accept digital payments; the revenue flows privately to payment processors, card companies, code-payment operators, and the point-of-sale and fintech firms that serve merchants.
stock_logic: The cashless share reached 58.0% in 2025, with an interim goal of 65% by 2030 and a longer-term goal of 80%. So the headline check is simply the annual cashless ratio. A second check is whether processing fees hold up or fall as volume grows, since fee levels decide how much of the shift turns into profit for the payment firms.

subcategory_name_en: New NISA & Household Savings-to-Investment Shift
subcategory_name_ja: 新NISA・貯蓄から投資へ
money_flow: This is not government spending; it is a tax incentive that changes where households put their money. By removing the tax on investment gains, it nudges savers to buy investment funds and shares. The flow lands with online brokers, asset managers, banks, and the stock exchange — they earn fees and trading volume as more household money moves in.
stock_logic: The early evidence is strong: NISA accounts reached about 24.3 million by mid-2024, and funds drew roughly ¥15 trillion of net inflows in 2024, more than double the prior year. That ¥15 trillion is household money, not a state outlay. The check going forward is whether inflows stay high after the initial rush, and whether the money goes into Japanese or foreign assets. Watch the monthly NISA inflow data.

subcategory_name_en: Asset Management Nation & Domestic Asset-Management Reform
subcategory_name_ja: 資産運用立国・国内アセットマネジメント改革
money_flow: This works through reform and market structure, not subsidy. Better-run, more trusted asset managers should attract more money to manage, which earns them fees. The beneficiaries are asset managers, trust banks, and the financial groups that own them, plus the data and custody firms that support the industry.
stock_logic: This is slower and softer than the NISA flow, because it depends on behavior changing, not money being handed out. Watch updates to the asset-management-center plan and any program to help new, independent managers get started. A useful check is whether assets under management at Japanese firms actually grow and whether foreign managers expand in Tokyo.

subcategory_name_en: TSE Cost-of-Capital, PBR & Capital Efficiency Reform
subcategory_name_ja: 東証 資本コスト・PBR・資本効率改革
money_flow: There is no government money here. The exchange applies pressure through disclosure: it publishes a monthly list of companies that have disclosed cost-of-capital actions, which pushes the rest to follow. The effect shows up as buybacks, higher dividends, sold-off cross-shareholdings, and balance sheets put to better use. The beneficiaries are the undervalued, cash-rich companies that respond — and their shareholders.
stock_logic: The pressure is real and ongoing. Watch the monthly TSE disclosure list and whether the exchange tightens expectations further. The deeper check is substance over form: not just whether a company publishes a plan, but whether return on equity actually rises and idle cash is actually returned. ('Below book' here means a price-to-book ratio under 1, measured against the company's reported equity.)

subcategory_name_en: Corporate Governance Code, Board Reform & Stewardship
subcategory_name_ja: コーポレートガバナンス・コード／取締役会改革・スチュワードシップ
money_flow: This works through codes and stewardship, not money. The Corporate Governance Code sets expectations for boards; the Stewardship Code asks investors to engage with the companies they own. Together they raise the chance that under-governed companies improve. The beneficiaries are governance-improvement candidates, the advisers and proxy firms that support change, and shareholders broadly.
stock_logic: The Stewardship Code was revised in June 2025 and a panel reviewing the Governance Code is active through 2025–2026. Watch the outcomes of that review and shareholder-meeting voting trends, which show whether investors are actually pressing for change. The substance check is independent-board upgrades and clearer board accountability, not just longer disclosures.

subcategory_name_en: Shareholder Returns, Buybacks & Balance-Sheet Optimization
subcategory_name_ja: 株主還元・自社株買い・バランスシート最適化
money_flow: No policy pays for this; the cash is the companies' own. Policy raises the probability of return rather than ordering it. Net-cash companies, low-return conglomerates sitting on idle assets, and banks unwinding old shareholdings are the names most likely to act. Shareholders receive the cash directly.
stock_logic: Buyback announcements across the market have hit records, so the trend is clear; the question is durability and breadth. Watch buyback and dividend announcements and treasury-share cancellations (cancelling bought-back shares, which permanently lifts per-share value). A check is whether payout ratios keep rising and whether returns spread from a few leaders to the broad market.

subcategory_name_en: Cross-Shareholding Reduction & Parent-Subsidiary Governance
subcategory_name_ja: 政策保有株式の縮減・親子上場ガバナンス
money_flow: No money changes hands from the state; the change is companies selling these stakes and redeploying the cash. Banks and insurers unwinding large cross-holdings free up capital for buybacks or investment, while listed subsidiaries and holding companies face pressure to simplify ownership. Shareholders of the sellers benefit as capital is freed.
stock_logic: The unwind is underway and disclosed. Watch the cross-shareholding reduction figures companies report, and restructurings of parent–subsidiary structures, including buyouts of listed subsidiaries. A clean check is the pace at which banks and insurers cut their strategic holdings, which they now disclose under pressure from the FSA and the exchange.

subcategory_name_en: Monetary Policy Normalization & Yield-Curve Repricing
subcategory_name_ja: 金融政策正常化・金利曲線の再評価
money_flow: This is monetary policy, not a budget. Higher interest rates let banks earn more on the gap between lending and deposit rates, and lift the investment income of insurers. At the same time, a higher cost of capital pressures the valuations of real estate and other rate-sensitive assets. So banks and insurers tend to benefit, while highly leveraged or asset-heavy businesses feel the squeeze.
stock_logic: The direction is set, but the pace is the variable. Watch the Bank of Japan's policy meetings and its guidance on the rate path. The key check is how far and how fast rates rise: a gradual rise lifts bank earnings without breaking borrowers, while a sharp rise would strain real estate and heavily indebted firms.

subcategory_name_en: Structural Wage Growth, Minimum Wage & Price Pass-Through
subcategory_name_ja: 構造的賃上げ・最低賃金・価格転嫁
money_flow: This is not a payment from the state; it is a mandate plus persuasion. The minimum wage is set higher each year, which lifts pay at the bottom directly. The flow-through to companies is mixed: labor-heavy businesses face higher costs, while firms that can raise prices or automate protect their margins. Retailers, restaurants, and staffing firms are the most exposed.
stock_logic: The FY2025 weighted-average minimum wage rose 6.26% to ¥1,121 an hour, and the government has signalled a path toward ¥1,500. The annual minimum-wage council decision each summer and the spring wage round are the key signals. The real check is whether companies can raise prices enough to cover the higher pay without losing customers — pay rises without price pass-through squeeze margins.

subcategory_name_en: SME Productivity, Labor-Saving Investment & Automation Subsidies
subcategory_name_ja: 中小企業の生産性・省力化投資・自動化補助
money_flow: The 2025 New Capitalism plan includes a labor-saving investment promotion plan: subsidies, often from a catalog of approved equipment, that cover part of the cost when a small firm automates. The money reaches makers of robots, point-of-sale systems, warehouse automation, and the systems integrators that install them, with the small firms as the buyers.
stock_logic: This is a structural, repeating demand rather than a one-time budget, because the labor shortage is permanent. Watch take-up of the labor-saving subsidies and the catalog's expansion. A grounded check is the order trends at automation and equipment makers, which show whether subsidized demand is actually converting into purchases.

subcategory_name_en: Part-Time Work, Social Insurance Reform & the “Annual Income Barrier”
subcategory_name_ja: パート労働・社会保険改革・年収の壁
money_flow: This is a rule change with some support spending attached, not a large outlay. Removing the barrier should bring more part-time hours into the workforce, easing labor shortages in shops, restaurants, and care. The beneficiaries are the labor-heavy employers that gain available hours, and the payroll and staffing firms that manage the change.
stock_logic: The government has said the ¥1.06 million insurance-contribution threshold is scheduled to be abolished within three years from June 2025, and company-size thresholds will be phased down. Watch the reform timeline and whether part-time hours actually rise. The check is labor-participation data for women and part-time workers.

subcategory_name_en: Women’s Labor Participation, Childcare & Work-Family Support
subcategory_name_ja: 女性の労働参加・子育て・仕事と家庭の両立
money_flow: This is real, recurring government spending. The money flows to families as cash (child allowance), to childcare providers as expanded capacity and benefits, and to the services families use. The investable beneficiaries are childcare operators, education and family-service firms, staffing for nurseries, and consumer names exposed to dual-income households.
stock_logic: The ¥3.6 trillion is committed and rolling out. Watch the child-allowance expansion and the build-out of childcare capacity. The harder check is effect rather than spending: whether the support actually lifts the birth rate or family incomes, which past programs across rich countries have struggled to do.

subcategory_name_en: Universal / Flexible Childcare & “Kodomo Dare Demo Tsuen”
subcategory_name_ja: こども誰でも通園制度（柔軟な保育）
money_flow: This is direct service spending within the Children's Future Strategy. The government pays for the new childcare hours, which flows to nursery operators, the staff they hire, and the local-government and software systems that run the bookings. Families gain access; providers gain a new funded demand stream.
stock_logic: The system is being institutionalized in FY2025 and becomes a nationwide benefit from FY2026. Watch the FY2026 national launch and how fast capacity is built to meet it. The practical check is whether there are enough nursery places and staff — a funded benefit with no capacity is a promise, not a service.

subcategory_name_en: Work-Style Reform, Overtime Caps & 2024 Problem Sectors
subcategory_name_ja: 働き方改革・時間外上限・2024年問題業種
money_flow: This is a regulation, not a spending program, and its effect runs through prices and productivity. With fewer legal hours, these sectors must either raise prices, consolidate, or automate. So logistics, construction, and medical-workflow firms that help cut hours gain pricing power or demand, while those that cannot adapt face higher costs.
stock_logic: The caps are in force, so the question is how the affected sectors respond. Watch enforcement and guideline updates, and the pricing and consolidation moves in trucking and construction. The clearest check is freight rates and construction-bid prices, which show whether the hour limits are translating into pricing power.

subcategory_name_en: Logistics, Transport Labor Shortages & Last-Mile Productivity
subcategory_name_ja: 物流・運輸の人手不足・ラストワンマイル生産性
money_flow: The mechanism is pricing and efficiency, backed by new rules. The government set standard freight rates and made shippers responsible for reducing drivers' waiting time, which strengthens carriers' ability to charge more. The beneficiaries are parcel and trucking firms with pricing power, warehouse-automation makers, and rail and shipping operators that absorb diverted freight.
stock_logic: This is the clearest line from a labor rule to company earnings. Watch standard-freight-rate increases and the enforcement of shipper responsibility. The check is whether carriers' yields (revenue per delivery) actually rise and whether warehouse-automation orders grow, which would show the squeeze converting into investment and pricing.

subcategory_name_en: Medical Fee Revision & Provider Reimbursement
subcategory_name_ja: 診療報酬改定・医療機関の収入
money_flow: The schedule governs how public health-insurance money (and patient co-pays) flows to providers. A revision that raises fees lifts provider revenue across the board; one that holds fees down squeezes it. For FY2026 the government raised the core treatment fees by 3.09% — the first 3%-plus rise in three decades — to help hospitals cope with inflation and pay staff more.
stock_logic: Because the schedule moves on a fixed two-year cycle, the timing is predictable; the size is what matters. The FY2026 rise is unusually large, which helps providers and the firms that supply them. Watch the next revision cycle and the deliberations of the council (Chuikyo) that sets it. The check is whether higher fees actually reach staff wages and provider margins, which the revision was designed to do.

subcategory_name_en: Drug Pricing, Reimbursement & Pharma Margin Pressure
subcategory_name_ja: 薬価・償還・医薬品マージン圧力
money_flow: This works against pharma earnings rather than for them. Lower official prices mean less revenue per pill for the same sales volume, squeezing makers of mature, off-patent-leaning drugs and the wholesalers that distribute them. Innovative, newly launched medicines are partly shielded, so the impact depends heavily on a company's product mix.
stock_logic: For FY2026 the government cut drug prices by 0.8%, and is widening the use of cost-effectiveness reviews for expensive drugs. Watch the annual drug-price revision and changes to the rules that speed older drugs toward generic-level pricing. The check is each company's exposure to mature versus newly launched products — the mix decides who is hurt.

subcategory_name_en: Healthcare DX, Electronic Medical Records & Nationwide Data Platform
subcategory_name_ja: 医療DX・電子カルテ・全国医療情報プラットフォーム
money_flow: This is a public buildout delivered through procurement. The government pays IT firms to build and connect the systems, which flows to electronic-medical-record vendors, hospital IT integrators, cloud and cybersecurity providers, and healthcare-data platforms. Hospitals and clinics adopt the systems, often with support to do so.
stock_logic: It is one of the clearest public healthcare-IT pipelines, with set milestones. Watch the electronic-medical-record standardization deadlines and the rollout of the nationwide platform and electronic prescriptions. The check is adoption: whether providers actually connect to the platform on schedule, since government IT timelines often slip.

subcategory_name_en: National Resilience, Disaster Prevention & Climate Adaptation Infrastructure
subcategory_name_ja: 国土強靱化・防災・気候適応インフラ
money_flow: This is direct, large-scale public works. The government adopted a new five-year plan to spend more than ¥20 trillion between fiscal 2026 and 2030, with about half going to lifeline infrastructure like water and sewage, ¥5.8 trillion to disaster-prevention works, and ¥1.8 trillion to regional and public-private resilience. The money flows to general contractors, civil-engineering and materials firms, and equipment makers.
stock_logic: This is one of the most durable spending cycles in the map, renewed every five years. The ¥20 trillion-plus is committed for FY2026–2030. Watch the annual budget allocations and any post-disaster supplementary budgets, which add to the base. The check is contractor order backlogs, which show the spending converting into work.

subcategory_name_en: Aging Infrastructure Maintenance, Bridges, Tunnels & Roads
subcategory_name_ja: 老朽インフラ維持管理（橋梁・トンネル・道路）
money_flow: The work is funded through public-works budgets, including the resilience program above. By law, road managers must inspect bridges and tunnels every five years, and nearly half of Japan's bridges will be over 50 years old within a decade. That creates a predictable, repeating stream of inspection and repair work for maintenance contractors, inspection and sensor firms, and repair-materials makers.
stock_logic: Because inspection is mandated on a five-year cycle, demand is unusually visible and recurring. Watch the inspection cycles and repair-and-renewal budget allocations. The check is the order backlogs at the firms that specialize in infrastructure repair, which turn the aging-asset problem into revenue.

subcategory_name_en: i-Construction 2.0, Construction Automation & Infrastructure DX
subcategory_name_ja: i-Construction 2.0・建設自動化・インフラDX
money_flow: This is a productivity policy backed by standards and procurement preference rather than a big subsidy. As public-works projects adopt these methods, demand flows to construction-equipment makers with machine-control technology, surveying and drone firms, and the software that runs 3D site data. Contractors that adopt the tools win work and protect margins as labor gets scarcer.
stock_logic: The policy is set; the question is adoption speed. Watch the rollout of i-Construction 2.0 requirements in public tenders and the uptake of automated and remote-controlled machinery. The check is the sales of machine-control and construction-software products, which show whether sites are actually automating.

subcategory_name_en: Logistics 2024 Problem, Trucking Reform & Freight Productivity
subcategory_name_ja: 物流2024年問題・トラック改革・貨物生産性
money_flow: The mechanism is pricing and efficiency, set by new rules rather than spending. The government published standard freight rates and made shippers responsible for cutting drivers' waiting time, which strengthens carriers' power to charge more. The beneficiaries are parcel and trucking firms with scale, warehouse-automation makers, logistics-property developers, and rail and shipping operators absorbing diverted freight.
stock_logic: This is the same force as the labor-side '2024 problem', viewed through infrastructure and freight. Watch standard-freight-rate increases, shipper-responsibility enforcement, and modal-shift incentives. The check is whether freight rates and carrier yields actually rise, and whether warehouse-automation orders grow.

subcategory_name_en: Child Allowance, Birth Support & Family Income Transfers
subcategory_name_ja: 児童手当・出産支援・家族向け所得移転
money_flow: This is a direct transfer — government cash to families. Households spend it on the things children need, so the flow reaches baby and children's-goods makers, education and childcare services, family restaurants and retailers, and consumer-finance and insurance firms. The effect is broad rather than concentrated, lifting spending across family-oriented consumption.
stock_logic: The expansion is funded within the ¥3.6 trillion package and rolling out. Watch the timing of the child-allowance expansion and how families actually spend it. The honest check is whether the extra cash lifts consumption or birth rates — past cash programs elsewhere have boosted spending more reliably than they have raised births.

subcategory_name_en: Inbound Tourism: Strategic Visitor Growth & Consumption Capture
subcategory_name_ja: インバウンド観光・戦略的訪日客拡大・消費取り込み
money_flow: This is demand creation rather than government spending. Visitors arrive and spend, and that money flows to airlines, airports, railways, hotels, department stores, drugstores, restaurants, and the payment firms that handle it. Policy supports the flow with promotion, easier travel, and (increasingly) a tourism levy to fund infrastructure, rather than direct subsidies to companies.
stock_logic: A record 42.7 million visitors came in 2025 and spent about ¥9.5 trillion; the 2030 target is 60 million visitors and ¥15 trillion of spending. The headline check is the monthly arrival and spending data from the tourism agencies. A second check is spending per visitor, since the policy is shifting from raw numbers toward higher-value, regionally dispersed tourism.

subcategory_name_en: Cashless Payments, Merchant Digitization & Consumption Data
subcategory_name_ja: キャッシュレス決済・加盟店DX・消費データ
money_flow: This is a target-and-standards policy, not spending. The government sets goals and encourages merchants to accept digital payments; the revenue flows privately to payment processors, card companies, code-payment operators, and the point-of-sale and retail firms that adopt them.
stock_logic: The cashless share reached 58.0% in 2025, against an interim goal of 65% by 2030 and a long-term goal of 80%. The headline check is the annual cashless ratio. A second check is processing-fee levels, since fees decide how much of the growing volume turns into profit for payment firms rather than savings for merchants.

subcategory_name_en: Food Security, Self-Sufficiency & Supply Resilience
subcategory_name_ja: 食料安全保障・自給率・供給強靱化
money_flow: Policy works through the agriculture ministry's budget and a web of subsidies and targets rather than one headline cheque. The money and support reach food producers, grain handlers and trading houses, food wholesalers, cold-chain and logistics firms, and the makers of farm inputs. A flagship target is to roughly double agriculture, forestry, fishery, and food exports to ¥5 trillion by 2030.
stock_logic: This is the top-level anchor for the whole food category, so it is best read as the frame for the more specific food sub-themes. Watch the implementation of the 2025 Basic Plan and the self-sufficiency and export targets. The check is whether domestic production capacity and exports actually grow, or whether import dependence keeps rising.

subcategory_name_en: Smart Agriculture, Robotics & Labor-Saving Farm Tech
subcategory_name_ja: スマート農業・ロボット・省力化農業技術
money_flow: The agriculture ministry funds and promotes adoption of these tools. The money and demand reach makers of agricultural machinery, drones and sensors, satellite-positioning and robotics firms, and farm-software providers. As farms consolidate into larger units, the case for buying this equipment strengthens.
stock_logic: This is a structural, demand-creating theme tied to the unavoidable aging of the farm workforce. Watch the smart-agriculture promotion measures and the adoption of autonomous machinery. The check is the sales of agricultural machinery and farm-management tools, which show whether the technology is actually being put to work on farms.

subcategory_name_en: Sustainable Inputs: Pesticide Reduction, Organic Farming & Bio-Inputs
subcategory_name_ja: 持続可能な投入（農薬削減・有機農業・バイオ資材）
money_flow: This works mainly through targets, certification, and support for alternatives rather than a single large subsidy. As the rules and targets tighten, demand shifts toward bio-pesticides, biofertilizers, organic producers, testing and certification firms, and the agri-tech that lets farmers use fewer chemicals. Food manufacturers also invest in automation to meet productivity goals.
stock_logic: These are 2050 goals, so progress is gradual and worth checking against the targets rather than assuming delivery. The headline targets are organic farming on 1 million hectares (25% of farmland), a 50% cut in risk-weighted chemical-pesticide use, a 30% cut in chemical fertilizer, and at least a 30% productivity gain in food manufacturing by 2030. Watch the milestone reviews.

subcategory_name_en: Food Exports, Japan Food Brands & Inbound Food Consumption
subcategory_name_ja: 農林水産物・食品輸出・日本食ブランド・インバウンド食消費
money_flow: This is promotion and target-setting rather than direct subsidy. The government promotes 'Japan brand' food, supports export logistics, and sets goals; the revenue flows to food and beverage makers with strong brands, seafood and seasoning exporters, packaging and cold-chain firms, and the trading houses that move the goods.
stock_logic: The 2025 Basic Plan targets ¥5 trillion of agriculture, forestry, fishery, and food exports by 2030, plus ¥4.5 trillion of food-related spending by foreign visitors in Japan. Watch export progress against that ¥5 trillion target. The check is the overseas-sales growth of the leading branded food and beverage makers, which shows whether the target is translating into actual demand.

subcategory_name_en: Circular Economy, Recycling Infrastructure & Resource Autonomy
subcategory_name_ja: サーキュラーエコノミー・リサイクルインフラ・資源自律
money_flow: Policy combines regulation (rules that require recycling and better design) with a growth strategy that supports the businesses doing the work. The money and demand reach waste-management and recycling firms, metals-recovery and materials companies, sorting-equipment makers, and the industrial-services firms that run the loops.
stock_logic: The government wants the domestic circular-economy market to grow from about ¥50 trillion in 2020 to ¥80 trillion in 2030 and ¥120 trillion by 2050 — so the size, not just the principle, is the target. Watch the resource-autonomy strategy measures and recycling-capacity additions. The check is the revenue growth of recycling and metals-recovery firms toward that ¥80 trillion 2030 market.
